articles.title: World Cup 2026: Best goal, player, fans and moments - vote for yours
articles.text:
Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Ian Dennis, BBC Radio 5 Live

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

John Murray, BBC Radio 5 Live

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Ian Dennis, BBC Radio 5 Live

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

John Murray, BBC Radio 5 Live

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

Ian Dennis, BBC Radio 5 Live

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

John Murray, BBC Radio 5 Live

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

Ian Dennis, BBC Radio 5 Live

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

John Murray, BBC Radio 5 Live

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

John Murray, BBC Radio 5 Live

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Ian Dennis, BBC Radio 5 Live

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.
articles.description: Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player. Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner. Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old...

articles.title: Marcus Rashford: What does the future hold for Manchester United forward?
articles.text:
Marcus Rashford's World Cup ended with a considered and thoughtful social media post, issued in the aftermath of England's amazing 6-4 third-place play-off victory over France in Miami.

Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep believing in us. Our time will come."

He added: "Now it's time for me to rest, reflect, spend some quality time with family and friends, and come back a better version of myself."

'Reflect' is the big word in that overview.

At 28, Rashford is moving into what should be the prime years of his career.

He has just won his first league title, La Liga with Barcelona, has 77 England caps and featured in six out of eight games as Thomas Tuchel's side delivered the country's best World Cup performance since 1966.

But in reality, a large part of what Rashford will be reflecting on with his family - some of whom are also key figures in terms of guiding his career - is whether his club future lies at Manchester United.

Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.

Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.

As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.

There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.

Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.

With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.

Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.

Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.

For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.

Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa.

Then the head coach put Rashford in his 'bomb squad', along with Alejandro Garnacho, Antony, Tyrell Malacia and Jadon Sancho, when pre-season training began last summer.

The other four players have all now left the club.

Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.

Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality.

He is so far yet to be asked what his stance is around the player returning to the fold.

For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants.

It is not clear what interest there is in Rashford at the Nou Camp given Barca have already signed England wide-man Anthony Gordon and are about to bring in Germany forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund.

The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line.

That financial discipline may have consequences - while the feeling was Elliot Anderson would have joined Manchester City anyway, United did not countenance paying the £116m Nottingham Forest eventually sold him for.

The same is true of Mateus Fernandes and his £85m switch from West Ham to Tottenham.

Nevertheless, Manchester United's enhanced used of data through director Michael Sansoni and his dedicated team means the club feel bullish about their chances of landing alternative targets who offer better value.

Clearly just as United need active sellers to buy from, they need buyers so they can offload those players they do not want.

In Rashford's case, there are a limited number of clubs globally who could match his current salary. And where a club are interested, the player has to want to join them.

Judging by his recent social media post, he would not do anything to jeopardise his England place.

BBC Sport has also been told of some Premier League clubs he would not be keen to move to.

So where will Rashford be when the transfer window closes on 1 September? Currently, the likeliest answer, by some distance, is Manchester United.

After that: Any Spanish club, any Turkish club, any German club. The vagueness there underlines the current lack of market for the 15th highest scorer in United's history, with 138 goals - someone who is only 17 short of a place in the top 10.

There are plenty, including former captain Gary Neville, who feel Rashford's time at Old Trafford is over and there should be no reconciliation.

In the end though, it may have to happen - and not everyone would be disappointed.

"A fit and focused Marcus Rashford has the ability to be one of the best players," said Rashford's friend Sam Smith - the Wrexham forward who scored the winner in their pre-season victory over Carrick's team in Helsinki on Saturday - when speaking to the United We Stand podcast.

"He has everything.

"He has had a difficult time at United and getting away from it all was a good thing.

"I don't know what is going to happen but as his friend, I hope he comes back and proves how good a player he was, how good he can be and starts scoring goals again."

Disappointments are guaranteed in football, he said, before opening out in a more positive vein and stating firmly: "Keep...

articles.title: World Cup 2026: Could Gianni Infantino push through a 64-team tournament?
articles.text:
If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.

More tournaments, more games, more money.

The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.

And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.

But why stop there?

Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup.

"If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.

Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.

Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense.

The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.

But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?

Let's start with how we got here.

The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.

The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.

Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.

In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.

Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".

There has been little support from elsewhere.

Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.

Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".

Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".

None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.

A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.

It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde.

Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive.

Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.

But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament.

Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.

Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.

Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.

Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):

AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).

Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).

Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).

Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).

OFC: New Caledonia (151).

Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).

Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.

And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year.

Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through.

New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.

There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.

But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.

Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.

Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present.

Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament?

Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.

But more groups means more time.

This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.

But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.

Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.

That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.

Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.

Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.

The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.

Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.

It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia.

Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?

How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?

Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.

This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.

There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations.

Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.

Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.

One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.

Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.

Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.

It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.

This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.

In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).

In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.

A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.

Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.

But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.
articles.description: If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better. More tournaments, more games, more money. The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices. And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the...
articles.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

articles.title: The Open 2026: How fast Fox trumped divisive DeChambeau to win at Royal Birkdale
articles.text:
Commentating on the thrilling climax of this year's Open, there was barely a moment to describe the biggest putt of Ryan Fox's life because he struck it so quickly.

There were only 22 seconds between Sam Burns completing his final round and Fox stroking home the 11-footer on Royal Birkdale's 18th green. The putt made him champion golfer of the year.

It provided a magnificent and wholesome climax to the 154th Open, a dramatic championship marked by class and controversy.

Bryson DeChambeau's two-shot penalty for trampling rough that improved the line of his swing path last Friday will not be forgotten. It was another incident that illustrated why he has become golf's most divisive figure.

But something else that should live long in the memory is Fox's fantastic triumph, a victory for a late developer who plays the game at a wonderful pace.

"He proved you can win The Open without all the faffing about," was a succinct assessment by our 5 Live editor on site, and it is hard to disagree.

It was a joy to walk all 18 holes of Sunday's final round with Fox and the time-efficient Burns playing at such a brisk pace.

"See shot, hit shot. That's always been my philosophy," Fox told BBC Sport while clutching the Claret Jug.

Of course, the new champion is exceptional and it would be wholly unrealistic to expect other players to compete at his pace.

But there are plenty of slowcoaches for whom the prescribed 40 seconds (50 if you are first to play) allowed for hitting is often ignored. The game could do with them trying harder to get closer to Fox's example.

"I played with Scottie Scheffler earlier in the year," said Fox. "At Phoenix, and we're in a three-ball on the Saturday.

"We walk off the ninth tee, and he goes, Foxy, it doesn't even feel like you're in the group. It just feels like we're playing in a two-ball, you hit it so fast."

Fox contends it is not difficult for him to play efficiently. "I just see the shot quickly," he said. "Pick a shot quickly, and the less time I have to think about it, the less bad stuff can creep in there."

Potentially damaging doubts were held at bay in weekend rounds of 62 and 68 capped by the quick-fire putt that sealed the championship.

It was the first time since Tom Watson in 1977 that a 72nd green birdie had secured golf's oldest and grandest title by a single shot.

This was quite the moment to elevate the sun-kissed event above the DeChambeau controversy which had threatened to overshadow the week.

Views varied among players as to whether he had breached Rule 8.1 by being heavy-footed around the area of his ball in the long grass to the right of the fifth hole during Friday's second round.

Several American golfers stood up for the LIV player, prime among them Scheffler, who insisted that DeChambeau is "not a cheater".

But this was not an issue over whether he deliberately cheated. The issue was whether he had broken a rule and officials deemed that it was a "clear-cut" breach.

DeChambeau's angry reaction, delaying signing his card and therefore denying the rest of the field knowledge of their Saturday tee times was not a good look.

And there was a report that DeChambeau threatened to get US President Donald Trump involved in his 30-minute conversation with rules officials.

The friends have played golf together and Trump had already been involved in one high-profile sporting controversy in July, when he controversially asked football governing body Fifa to review United States striker Folarin Balogun's one-match suspension at the World Cup.

Balogun's ban was suspended, allowing him to play in their last-16 defeat by Belgium.

When I asked R&A chief executive Mark Darbon on Saturday if Trump had been in touch, he replied that he had not.

DeChambeau continues to divide opinion. His legions of fans will feel he was hard done by and it was remarkable to witness the warm reception he received on Saturday after he had been docked those two shots.

He does things his own way and there are times, like last week, when that makes him appear self-absorbed - a trait that does not usually play well in a golf locker room.

Rory McIlroy made it abundantly clear, even if saying that he is "not fond" of the two time US Open champion is probably a considerable understatement.

DeChambeau blanked the media all week despite being in contention and in the spotlight.

Perhaps we will hear from him at LIV's event at the JCB Club in Staffordshire later this week, when the reporting contingent will be significantly smaller. Or maybe his views will be kept for his self curated YouTube channel.

He is as unique as his uniform length clubs and undoubtedly brings much needed character to the game.

But so too do figures such as Fox. Yes, he comes from New Zealand sporting royalty, the son of All Blacks legend Grant Fox and grandson of former Kiwi cricket captain Merv Wallace but he is a self made star.

The new Open champion was 24 when he turned professional and has become a major winner only after going through the hard yards of an unforgiving pro game.

"My second year on the Aussie Tour, I was pretty close to giving it up," Fox said. "I remember getting to the Aussie Masters at Royal Melbourne.

"It was the second-to-last event of the year. I had just missed the cut at the Aussie PGA badly. My dad came over and caddied for me, and he said to me, why do you play this game?

"And I said, well, it's supposed to be fun. But it wasn't really very fun at the time. He goes, well, let's just go out this week and try to have some fun."

They did. Fox finished fifth, kept his card and clung on to a path that brought wins on the DP World Tour, PGA Tour and now the Open Championship.

In contrast to DeChambeau, he is a unanimously popular figure among his peers. And his pacy brand of golf is great fun to watch.

There were only 22 seconds between Sam Burns completing his final round and Fox stroking home the 11-footer on Royal Birkdale's 18th green. The putt...

articles.title: World Cup 2026: How has the World Cup affected your Premier League team?
articles.text:
Now Spain have been crowned world champions after victory over Argentina in New Jersey, attention will quickly shift to the new domestic season.

The Premier League was well represented in North America, with all 20 of the top-flight teams for 2026-27 having at least one player involved.

From injuries to transfers, here is how the World Cup has impacted your Premier League team.

Arsenal may be the Premier League team affected most by the World Cup.

The Gunners face an anxious wait over the fitness of William Saliba, who had been managing a back issue before his tournament was ended by the same problem.

The centre-back has not yet been assessed by the club's medical team, but if he was sidelined, it would be a big loss - especially as his defensive partnership with Gabriel is so crucial to how the team play.

The other two question marks will be over the fitness of Declan Rice and Bukayo Saka, who helped England finish third while managing their own fitness issues.

It does not look like they will need a longer lay-off once the tournament is over, but they will be monitored closely by Arsenal before the new season.

Leandro Trossard enjoyed a fine tournament with Belgium, starting all six games and scoring twice as they reached the quarter-finals, before the forward joined Turkish club Besiktas for £15m.

Gabriel Martinelli, who showed flashes of his quality playing for Brazil, is another player the Gunners could be open to selling this summer.

Carlo Ancelotti played the winger in a central role during the tournament, and that may be an extra attraction for potential buyers.

Aston Villa's transfer plans were thrown a curveball by an injury to Amadou Onana.

The midfielder ruptured the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in his right knee during Belgium's last-16 win over the USA which will rule him out until next year.

They were not planning significant changes in midfield, although an extra body would have been useful, so moved for Johan Manzambi and Joao Gomes - especially with Youri Tielemans joining Manchester United.

Manzambi - who has completed a club record move for more than £50m from Freiburg - excelled for Switzerland, scoring three goals, and will form part of Villa's new-look squad as Unai Emery tries to refresh it amid potential further sales.

Chelsea have agreed a £117m deal to sign England midfielder Morgan Rogers, while France's Lucas Digne is due to join Paris St-Germain, but goalkeeper Emi Martinez, whose Argentina side lost to Spain in the final, is likely to stay.

Bournemouth fans will have been encouraged by the performances of Ben Gannon-Doak at the World Cup, despite Scotland's brief stay in the tournament. The winger played in all three group matches, and particularly impressed in their sole win over Haiti.

The Cherries had six players at the World Cup, including New Zealand back-up goalkeeper Alex Paulsen who has joined Motherwell on loan for the coming season.

Starring among them, as well as Gannon-Doak, was winger Rayan. The 19-year-old started all Brazil's games bar their opener and claimed an assist in the 3-0 win over Scotland to seal their path to the knockout stage.

It further raises Rayan's stock following his January move to Bournemouth, and superb performances in the second half of last season.

This could be bad news for Bournemouth though, with Rayan heavily linked with Liverpool, managed by former Cherries boss Andoni Iraola.

For a couple of Brentford players, the World Cup could not have gone much worse.

Striker Igor Thiago came into the tournament on the back of a stellar scoring season for the Bees and tipped as Brazil's lead attacker in North America. However, after a lacklustre performance in the opener against Morocco he was dropped by manager Carlo Ancelotti and did not appear again.

It remains to be seen what impact this has on Thiago's confidence heading into the new season.

Meanwhile, midfielder Jordan Henderson made one substitute appearance for England, but his most memorable contribution was falling over an advertising hoarding during the celebrations following the win over Mexico and requiring surgery on a broken wrist.

Henderson, however, stayed with the England squad and should be fit for the start of Brentford's season, though he may have to initially play in a cast.

Brighton went into the World Cup with eight representatives and emerged with eight - although the personnel has changed.

Dutch centre-back Jan Paul van Hecke has moved to Tottenham from Brighton, with Croatia defender Luka Vuskovic going the other way.

Vuskovic endured a difficult World Cup, subbed off after 66 minutes of Croatia's opening loss to England, then not appearing in their three subsequent games.

Indeed, the only one of Brighton's eight representatives to make it past the last 16 was Belgium left-back Maxim de Cuyper who played in all six of their matches, starting five.

This could be useful for Brighton, who will hope key players like Bart Verbruggen, Pascal Gross and Yasin Ayari will therefore not be too tired after the World Cup and fresh for the start of the Premier League season.

Chelsea had 11 players involved at the World Cup, but only seven played in the tournament.

Key players Cole Palmer, Levi Colwill and Joao Pedro were left out of their respective squads and have already returned for pre-season training.

The greatest concern has been the fitness of captain Reece James, who played into the latter stages of the tournament after helping England reach the third-place play-off, but suffered hamstring issues.

Enzo Fernandez, who was sent off in the final against Spain, and Malo Gusto are set to return late for pre-season, along with expected new signing Valentin Barco.

England midfielder Morgan Rogers is set to arrive at Chelsea from Aston Villa for that club record £117m deal, though international team-mate Trevoh Chalobah is expected to leave this summer.

Much like Cristiano Ronaldo after Portugal knocked England out of the European Championship in 2006, Fernandez may find his antics in and around Argentina's victory over England will make him a target for opposition fans next season should he stay with Chelsea.

New Coventry defender Aurele Amenda suffered World Cup frustration. The centre-back, who has joined from Frankfurt, was an unused substitute in Switzerland's run to the quarter-finals.

Striker Haji Wright played against Australia and Belgium as co-hosts USA bowed out to the Red Devils in the last 16.

The 28-year-old will be one of Coventry's main threats as they return to the Premier League for the first time in 25 years, having scored 20 goals to help them to the Championship title last season.

Fellow forward Brandon Thomas-Asante, who scored 13 goals for the Sky Blues last season, also featured in two of Ghana's World Cup games as they exited the competition in the last 32.

Iliman Ndiaye is one of Everton's biggest assets and was at the centre of transfer talk before the World Cup.

He made an impact at the tournament with a spectacular strike after coming on as a substitute in Senegal's 5-0 win against Iraq. They reached the last 32 before losing 3-2 to Belgium.

It remains to be seen whether the World Cup reignites speculation, but Everton will hope it stays quiet as Ndiaye is arguably their most creative player.

Idrissa Gueye, Ndiaye's Senegal team-mate, had his Everton deal expire at the start of July, so is effectively a free agent.

All talks on his future were shelved until the end of the World Cup, so a decision on the 36-year-old's next move will be made soon.

Nathan Patterson played for Scotland at right-back, but he has failed to convince manager David Moyes that he should be a regular starter at Everton.

The Toffees are actively pursuing targets in that position, so Patterson could yet be on the move before the start of the season.

It was a World Cup to remember for four out of the six Fulham players involved.

Sander Berge and Oscar Bobb were part of the brilliant Norway side beaten by England in the quarter-finals, while Belgium's Timothy Castagne and Morocco's Issa Diop also fell at that stage.

While Bobb was not often a starter for Norway, his second-half appearances allowed him to showcase his talents and ability to move the ball in the final third.

But for Fulham fans, it was a chance to catch a glimpse of defender Luc de Fougerolles.

The 20-year-old has been at Craven Cottage since 2023 but is yet to make an appearance after spending last season on loan at Belgium Pro League side Dender EH.

Perhaps new manager Alvaro Arbeloa will be pleased with what he saw.

Newly-promoted Hull watched two players leave for the World Cup and welcomed home one.

Croatia goalkeeper Ivor Pandur made the switch from the Tigers to Rangers for a fee of £6m at the end of June.

But moving the other way from Ibrox was Jack Butland to fill the goalkeeping void.

Hull fans did have something to cheer as Liam Millar progressed to the last 16 of the World Cup in a historic run for co-hosts Canada.

Hull boss Sergej Jakirovic can look forward to having an almost completely fresh squad ready to tackle pre-season before the Premier League starts in August.

Like fellow newly promoted Hull, Ipswich only had two players make their way to America, Canada and Mexico for the World Cup.

Striker George Hirst was named in the first Scotland squad to reach the World Cup in 26 years, while Ali Al-Hamadi was there with Iraq.

Hirst failed to make it off the bench for Scotland in a campaign that ended at the group stage, as Al-Hamadi appeared three times for winless Iraq.

For new Ipswich manager Gary O'Neil, it presents a chance to put out his pre-season ideas to an almost full squad - including new signing Emersonn.

The striker is the first Brazilian to sign for the Tractor Boys but, despite playing for his country at youth level, he is yet to win a senior cap.

Leeds fans could only keep an eye on four of their players at the World Cup - with new signing Harry Wilson's Wales failing to qualify.

Left-back Gabriel Gudmundsson was the Leeds player who appeared the most times in the tournament, featuring in all four of Sweden's matches.

Midfielder Ao Tanaka was left heartbroken by Japan's stoppage-time loss to Brazil in the last 32. He made three appearances for his nation - who many thought were dark horses for the tournament.

Despite coming within a whisker of the semi-finals with Switzerland, Noah Okafor will return to Elland Road fresh, with just one World Cup appearance to his name.

Leeds signed defender Tarik Muharemovic for £34.1m from Serie A side Sassuolo in the summer. The 23-year-old made three appearances for Bosnia-Herzegovina in the World Cup, including in the last-32 defeat by co-hosts the United States.

With survival the aim for Daniel Farke's side last season, now it is all about progression.

Liverpool have a World Cup winner and runner-up in their squad after Spain beat Argentina in the final on Sunday.

New signing Victor Munoz did not play a minute for Spain at the tournament but has a winner's medal. Alexis Mac Allister excelled for Argentina after a below-par season at Anfield, and his effective display against England will certainly have pleased Iraola.

Aside from that, Liverpool will take plenty of confidence from the other players they had in North America. Alexander Isak scored once and got three assists for Sweden while Florian Wirtz provided three assists for Germany.

Cody Gakpo scored three times for the Netherlands, with Virgil van Dijk and Ryan Gravenberch also playing for them.

Meanwhile, Alisson played every minute for Brazil before they were knocked out by Norway in the last 16.

In terms of signings, Liverpool have expressed interest in Yan Diomande, while Bradley Barcola is also on their shortlist. Both impressed for Ivory Coast and France respectively.

The most concrete World Cup news for Manchester United was Manuel Ugarte's knee injury.

Ugarte is going to be out until 2027 after having surgery to repair cruciate ligament damage. Given the Uruguay midfielder was likely to be sold this summer, it has added an extra layer of complication around United's recruitment plans.

Then we have Kobbie Mainoo.

The 21-year-old shrugged off the disappointment at being continually overlooked by Ruben Amorim to deliver excellent performances over the second half of last season, which suggests he can bounce back from not playing for England at the World Cup.

However, Michael Carrick, who experienced the same thing under Fabio Capello in 2010, might need to put an arm around his shoulder.

Argentina defender Lisandro Martinez went off injured in the World Cup final with what looked like a muscle injury, and United will be hoping the damage is not serious when he is fully assessed.

Anthony Elanga and Yoane Wissa's performances for Sweden and DR Congo respectively will have offered Newcastle United a little encouragement.

The pair even scored as many goals combined (five) at the World Cup as they did during their challenging debut campaign at St James' Park.

Elsewhere, Newcastle captain Bruno Guimaraes may have missed a penalty in Brazil's last-16 defeat against Norway, but the midfielder still caught the eye by setting up four goals at the tournament.

It is easy to see why Newcastle do not want to lose their talisman after he intimated he wished to join Arsenal.

The sight of another midfielder, Johan Manzambi, impressing for Switzerland ultimately left Newcastle wondering what might have been after their top target opted to join Aston Villa instead.

Newcastle have targeted young players with a similar profile this summer, but head coach Eddie Howe will rely on experienced leaders like Dan Burn to help them along.

Burn, fresh from making his World Cup debut for England aged 34, still has a lot to offer.

Tottenham's Lucas Bergvall is a priority target for Nottingham Forest. They are looking to replace Elliot Anderson after his record move to Manchester City and Bergvall is their number one option.

The midfielder helped Sweden to the last 32 of the World Cup, where they lost 3-0 to France.

Winger Dan Ndoye was key in Switzerland's run to the quarter-finals and could flourish in Oliver Glasner's new team. He scored twice, including in Switzerland's quarter-final defeat by defending champions Argentina.

Austria reached the last 32 with Xaver Schlager featuring and the midfielder is expected to join Forest on a free transfer after leaving RB Leipzig.

Ibrahim Sangare helped Ivory Coast reach the last 32, where they lost to Norway, while Tyler Bindon and Chris Wood could not stop New Zealand exiting at the group stage.

Sunderland were among the Premier League clubs with the highest number of players participating at the World Cup and it is no surprise given they secured European football last season.

There were mixed results for the Black Cats contingent with captain Granit Xhaka narrowly missing out on a semi-final match against England.

Chemsdine Talbi was a bright spark for Morocco when used and Brian Brobbey proved as difficult to play against as ever when leading the line for the Netherlands.

Striker Wilson Isidor's journey ended at the group stage with Haiti but he will always have that stunning goal against Morocco to look back on.

To boost their already exciting squad, Sunderland have added experienced Belgium right-back Thomas Meunier to their ranks.

Tottenham Hotspur's main transfer business was done before the World Cup, with arrivals such as Sandro Tonali, Mateus Fernandes, Andrew Robertson, plus central defenders Marcos Senesi and Jan Paul van Hecke.

This has sparked talk of outgoings, which the tournament could have an impact on.

It is the third World Cup in succession that Spurs were represented in the final, with Hugo Lloris playing for France in 2018 and 2022, while Argentina's Cristian Romero made his second appearance.

The arrival of Senesi and Van Hecke has increased speculation about Spurs captain Romero's future, and his displays for Argentina will have further attracted the clubs who have been linked with him, including Atletico Madrid and Inter Milan.

Romero has the ultimate showcase when Argentina face Spain in the World Cup final. Another strong performance might bring a move closer, as well as increasing Spurs' bargaining position.

The Premier League was well represented in North America, with all 20 of the top-flight teams for 2026-27 having at least one player involved. From injuries to transfers,...

articles.title: World Cup 2026: Why David Beckham was face of the tournament in the US
articles.text:
In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham.

During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss.

On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi.

Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup, fronting advertising campaigns for 11 brands ranging from McDonald's and Major League Soccer to Stella Artois, Home Depot and Bank of America.

And if you haven't seen him during one of the many half-time commercial breaks, you've probably spotted him at the United States' opening match in Los Angeles, watching alongside his friend Tom Cruise.

Just hours before that, Beckham had become the first professional footballer to receive a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, with actor Cruise once again by his side.

More recently, he was seen singing Wonderwall and Hey Jude as he celebrated Jude Bellingham's two goals against Norway that sent England into the semi-finals.

"David speaks to so many different people. I think that's what's unique about him," said Stacy Jones, founder and CEO of the marketing agency Hollywood Branded.

"He brings in the female demographic because he is so sexy - but he appeals to men. He's an honest, good, authentic, down-to-earth type of individual. So you don't really get affronted by him."

She felt Beckham had achieved something few celebrities manage, moving effortlessly between football, fashion, entertainment, business and the cult of celebrity.

On Hermosa Beach, south of Los Angeles, hundreds of fans gathered to watch Spain reach the World Cup final at an event organised by LA Galaxy - the club Beckham joined in 2007 from Real Madrid.

Ask fans what comes to mind when they hear the name David Beckham and the answers come quickly.

"Victoria Beckham."

"The Spice Girls."

"The McDonald's advert."

"David Beckham was a really good player for England, but he's doing too many commercials now," said one football fan.

For Megan Hapeman, it's the recent McDonald's advert in which Beckham hopes to receive a cup featuring his own face.

Her six-year-old son Oliver proudly told me he still has one.

"When I got it, I was pretty proud," he said. "Because Mommy liked it, I liked it too."

That's exactly what brands are buying.

Beckham transcends clubs, generations and even football itself.

That broad appeal didn't happen by accident. It was built on Beckham's football legacy in America.

He remains the player with the trademark free-kicks, part of Manchester United's Treble-winning side in 1999, England captain and one of Real Madrid's original Galácticos.

Dan Courtemanche, Major League Soccer's executive vice-president and chief communications officer, admitted Beckham's influence on the game in America cannot be overstated.

"I always tell people he changed the fate of American soccer. Not once, but twice," he said.

When Beckham signed for LA Galaxy 19 years ago, stadiums across Major League Soccer sold out before he had even played a competitive match.

The league needed credibility and he gave it something even broader: mainstream celebrity.

Sixteen years later, Beckham helped persuade Argentina icon Messi to choose Inter Miami over rival offers globally - another moment that transformed MLS, according to Courtemanche.

"Lionel Messi decided to make Major League Soccer his league of choice and Inter Miami his club of choice," he added. "Again, it took us to a whole other level."

Tom Braun, LA Galaxy's president and chief operating officer, said Beckham's influence has stretched way beyond football.

"David's an iconic figure in our sport and our culture," he explained.

"He's built a business around his brand and continues to resonate with people."

The evolution of the 51-year-old, who also played for AC Milan and Paris St-Germain before retiring in 2013, was perhaps best symbolised this summer on Hollywood Boulevard.

When Beckham accepted his star on the Walk of Fame, he reflected he had never imagined "a working-class English footballer" would receive such an honour.

The recognition celebrated not only the life of Beckham the footballer, but Beckham the cultural figure.

"David Beckham, to America, is the World Cup," Jones said.

"He is soccer to us."

Having arrived in Los Angeles promising to grow the game, Beckham is still helping sell football to America.

Only now, he's doing it not with a free-kick, but with a brand.
articles.description: In America, nobody sells football quite like David Beckham. During this World Cup, the former England captain has been impossible to miss. On the pitch, the tournament belongs to stars like Kylian Mbappe, Jude Bellingham, Lamine Yamal and Lionel Messi. Off the pitch, Sir David has become the face of the World Cup,...

articles.title: World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC
articles.text:
Spain's victory over Argentina in Sunday's World Cup final attracted a peak audience of 15.8 million on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.

The BBC enjoyed a 64% share of all TV viewing for the match, with an average of 13.9m watching as Ferran Torres' extra-time strike sealed Spain's second World Cup triumph.

The final was streamed 11.1m times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, including 2.4m UHD streams on iPlayer.

The BBC Sport live text coverage on the website and app was viewed more than 18.8m times, including 14.2 million views in the UK, providing fans with the latest news, analysis and updates from the match.

Saturday's third-place play-off between England and France brought in a peak audience of 11.8m on BBC One and iPlayer, with an average audience of 10.8m.

Across the World Cup as a whole, the BBC saw an overall reach of 42.8 million across TV viewing.

Audiences streamed World Cup matches 237 million times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, more than double the 2022 World Cup, and football dominated BBC iPlayer, with World Cup content accounting for 33% of everything streamed during the competition.

Additionally, the BBC Sport website and app attracted 57.2 million unique visitors, with fans viewing BBC Sport content 1.7 billion times, match highlights of World Cup matches were streamed 67.3m times while the new BBC Football YouTube channel generated 100m views across a diverse range of content.

Across social media, clips, interviews, analysis, press conferences and fan moments attracted 2.55 billion video views.

The top-performing video of the World Cup was Egypt winger Mostafa Zico's interview after Egypt went out to Argentina, with 77 million views on TikTok alone.

Alex Kay-Jelski, Director of BBC Sport, said: "This has been a World Cup we'll never forget, and that's entirely down to the millions of people who chose to experience it with the BBC.

"Thank you to everyone who watched, streamed, listened, read, shared and joined us throughout the tournament.

"Whether you were watching at home, following on your phone during the working day, catching up on Football Daily or diving into our new interactive experiences, you made this a summer of football that brought the nation together."

The BBC enjoyed a 64% share of all TV viewing for the match, with an average of 13.9m watching as Ferran Torres' extra-time strike sealed Spain's second World Cup triumph. The final was...

articles.title: WTA to require tennis players to undergo genetic sex testing
articles.text:
The Women's Tennis Association (WTA) is to require all players to undergo genetic sex testing in order to compete on the WTA Tour.

In the latest edition of its women's eligibility policy, which will come into effect on Tuesday, players will be required to provide a cheek swab, blood or saliva sample to test for the presence of the SRY gene which is part of the Y chromosome and causes male characteristics to develop.

Players will be tested just once in their lifetime and only a negative result, showing no presence of the gene, will permit them to compete on the Tour.

If they test positive, players will face further medical assessment before being allowed to play.

The new policy also requires players to sign a document that states they could face disciplinary action if they refuse testing.

The WTA's previous policy, last updated in 2024, allowed transgender women to compete on the Tour if they had lowered their testosterone levels to below 2.5 nmol/L continuously for the previous two years.

There are no known transgender women currently playing on the WTA Tour.

"The WTA's women's eligibility policy is designed to promote equal athletic opportunities in women's professional tennis and maintain fair competition for all players participating in WTA tournaments," the governing body said.

"Since first being introduced in 1975, the eligibility policy has undergone periodic review in light of developments across international sport.

"Following the most recent review, which included consultation with WTA members and evolving standards across women's sport, the WTA board approved an updated WTA women's eligibility policy beginning in 2026 based on biological sex."

It added: "The WTA recognises that this is a sensitive and complex issue and is committed to treating all players with dignity and implementing the policy in a respectful and thoughtful way."

The policy change comes a year after World Athletics announced new regulations - brought into effect in September 2025 - requiring athletes wanting to compete in the female category for world ranking competitions to take a one-time gene test.
articles.description: The Women's Tennis Association (WTA) is to require all players to undergo genetic sex testing in order to compete on the WTA Tour. In the latest edition of its women's eligibility policy, which will come into effect on Tuesday, players will be required to provide a cheek swab, blood or saliva sample to test for the...

articles.title: Grace Ballinger column: How will a revamped Hundred play out?
articles.text:
Three days on from winning the T20 Blast title with The Blaze, Grace Ballinger, now of the Manchester Super Giants, takes a closer look at The Hundred which starts on Tuesday.

New team names, new kits, new owners - the 2026 edition of The Hundred is here!

Slightly earlier than last year, this edition of The Hundred kicks off in London on Tuesday, where two of the teams bought by IPL franchises, MI London and Sunrisers Leeds, will face each other at The Oval.

The Hundred looks different to how it has for the past few years, and nobody, players included, really knows how it will play out.

This is the first year of privatisation within the tournament. Whilst it was previously centrally owned by the ECB, the eight teams are now all owned independently, after the host counties had the option to sell either the entirety, or part, of their side to independent buyers.

This privatisation manifests itself in many ways, but the essence of the tournament has remained the same.

The eight host grounds have stayed, and some teams have kept their original names.

Many of the brands that have been built over the past few years, on the other hand, have been totally transformed.

When the privatisation was announced, I was not really sure how it would affect me as a player.

The one inarguable benefit for the players, especially in the female game, is the jump in salaries.

For instance, my salary last year was £16,000, and this summer it has increased to £42,500 for the tournament.

The only other thing that has been apparent so far, is instead of everything being centrally managed by the ECB, management is now under the control of the independent franchises.

For example, last year every kit was New Balance, whereas this year, there are a range of kit brands.

In many instances, the brands and squads that were built up over a period of years, disappeared, or rebranded, very quickly.

Sunrisers Leeds are an excellent example of this.

Formerly Northern Superchargers, the Headingley-based outfit rebranded to align to IPL side Sunrisers Hyderabad, following a £100m takeover by The Sun Group.

The kit, once purple, is now a bright orange.

Not only have they entirely revamped the image, but the players too.

Northern Superchargers was the side I played for during the last three editions of The Hundred. During this three years, the side barely changed, there were zero debutants in the opening game of last season.

Our women's side was successful, reaching the final in 2023 and winning the title in 2025.

However, not a single player from that winning side was bought by Sunrisers Leeds in the 2026 auction.

They retained three players in the original retention window, but instead chose to build an entirely different squad around them.

Sunrisers Leeds are not alone in this though. The nature of an auction meant players were inevitably going to be moved around the country.

My move is a simple crossing of the Pennines, after being picked up by Manchester Super Giants (formerly Manchester Originals) in the auction.

I am really excited to make the move to the Old Trafford side, and have enjoyed playing there across the years as a visiting player.

I feel they had a successful auction and appeared to have a good strategy, creating a well-balanced squad.

Personally, I also look forward to working under two experienced Australians - head coach Matthew Mott and captain Meg Lanning.

Mott has formerly led the extremely successful Australian women's side, and was also the England men's white-ball head coach when they lifted the 2022 T20 World Cup trophy.

Meanwhile, Lanning formerly captained the Australian national side, and herself has won seven world-cup titles.

In my opinion, one of the greatest things about franchise cricket is being able to work with, and learn from, different coaches and team-mates, and I'm sure working under Mott and Lanning alike will not only beneficial, but hopefully really enjoyable.

It is only myself and Kathryn Bryce from The Blaze heading up to the Super Giants, meaning the squad is mostly composed of players I have not been in a side with before.

This in itself is an opportunity I look forward to, I think it is good as a player to expose yourself to different team dynamics.

Team dynamics will be an interesting thing for all sides to work out. As I earlier alluded to, most of the squads are very different to how they looked last summer.

There is a very quick turnaround between the T20 Blast and the Hundred - a mere two days between the men's T20 Blast Finals Day and The Hundred's opening games.

There is therefore little time for warm-up games, which many Hundred teams have chosen to do in the past.

The first time you take the field with your team may well be in your opening game.

It is the same for all sides though, all teams are in the same position.

It may be that the sides that gel the quickest are at a slight advantage early in the tournament.

The Hundred, for all the controversy it has caused, has been an amazing part of my career so far.

Being part of a winning side last summer, and lifting a trophy in front of 22,000 people at Lords, is an incredibly special memory for myself and my family, and it is certainly something I will be looking to repeat this summer, albeit for a different team.

I have also bowled against some of the best players in the world for the past four years, which is something I just would not have been able to do at the age of 21 had it not been for this competition.

The tournament has put women's cricket on the map in England, and the strength of the domestic game has only grown year by year.

I hope this year's edition of the Hundred is something people will buy into, and bring their kids too.

The main thing I hope for, of course, is a Super Giants double!

New team names, new kits, new owners - the 2026 edition of The Hundred is here! Slightly earlier than last year, this edition of The Hundred...

articles.title: Bruno Mars brings hits, romance and a Paddington Bear to Wembley
articles.text:
Bruno Mars appeared determined to lift the nation's mood during the first of his six nights at Wembley Stadium.

His last UK performance came in July 2018, shortly after England had been knocked out of the men's World Cup semi-finals. Eight years later, history had repeated itself.

But wearing an England football scarf, Union Jack bucket hat and clutching a Paddington Bear, the showman energised a 75,000-strong, multi-generational crowd.

After nearly a decade away from UK audiences, Mars proved why fans had snapped up tickets in record numbers, external.

The show began with a pre-recorded video of Mars praying in a church for a good performance, before he emerged beneath visuals of a stained-glass window in a red suit bejewelled with roses.

It was a suitably theatrical opening to a two-hour, 15-minute set. But for those who had avoided spoilers, Risk It All, a slow R&B ballad from Mars' latest album, The Romantic, was perhaps an unexpected opener.

Any surprise was short-lived. Within minutes, Mars sat behind a pair of bright red congas for Cha Cha, signalling the start of a set that moved between R&B, funk, Latin influences and pop.

It was an early glimpse of what was to come as he declared: "London, it's time to party."

The crowd quickly accepted the invitation. From then on, neither Mars nor his fans stood still.

24K Magic and Treasure prompted one of the night's biggest singalongs. As Mars sang "put your pinky rings up to the moon", thousands of little fingers rose into the air.

The opening run of songs showed why Mars has remained one of pop's most reliable live performers. Rather than relying on one sound, he moved between genres without ever losing the crowd.

Romance has always been at the heart of the singer's music, and it soon became the focus of the show.

"Tonight we sing about love," he told the crowd after Treasure, before introducing the Angel Baby Cam, a segment in which fans' reactions were projected onto the giant screens as he serenaded them.

It was one of several moments that played into his trademark romantic showmanship.

Later, he even emerged wearing a cape emblazoned with the words The Romantic, which appeared to nod to soul legend James Brown.

In the years between his solo releases, Mars has focused on collaborations, most notably Silk Sonic with Anderson .Paak, a partnership he has previously described as "natural and organic"., external

That chemistry was evident as Anderson .Paak joined Mars on stage for a four-song Silk Sonic segment, with the pair delivering tightly choreographed Motown-inspired dance routines and playful banter.

This included their attempts at British accents as they declared the evening to be "absolutely wicked".

Silk Sonic was not the only collaboration to feature. Although Mars has recently enjoyed chart success with Die With a Smile, alongside Lady Gaga, and APT. with Rosé, only the former made the setlist.

Performed during the show's ballad section, Die With a Smile prompted one of the evening's biggest singalongs, suggesting the song has earned its place alongside Mars' more established hits.

As the show drew to a close, Mars returned to the songs that helped cement his place as one of pop's biggest stars.

For his 2010 hit Just the Way You Are, the singer asked for the stadium lights to be turned up so he could see the crowd, revealing thousands of fans singing every word back to him, before Uptown Funk transformed Wembley into a giant dancefloor complete with fireworks and gold confetti.

The show ended with Dance With Me, a ballad in which Mars yearns for one last slow dance to save a struggling relationship.

Like Risk It All at the start of the evening, it was an unexpected choice to end the concert.

It left the crowd on a reflective note, perhaps hoping they would not have to wait another eight years to see him again.

His last UK performance came in July 2018, shortly after England had been knocked out of the men's World Cup semi-finals. Eight years later, history had repeated itself. But wearing an England football...

articles.title: AliExpress gets record €550m fine from EU for allowing sale of illegal products
articles.text:
Chinese online retail giant AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£467m) by the EU for allowing the sale of illegal products such as unsafe toys and counterfeit clothes.

The penalty is the highest fine imposed under the Digital Services Act, which requires tech giants to do more to counter illegal and harmful content.

The European Commission said AliExpress fell short of its obligation under the act to "diligently assess" the risk of illegal, unsafe or fake goods on its platform.

The company said the fine was disproportionate and that it has made enhancements to its processes.

The two year investigation found that AliExpress's detection systems "did not work properly", with many illegal products not flagged while others that were identified remained on the site for several weeks.

The European Commission also found the company did not properly enforce penalties on traders selling illegal goods.

Furthermore, its product compliance checks could be "easily circumvented".

AliExpress, which is owned by the Chinese tech conglomerate Alibaba, has 193 million users in Europe, more than fellow Chinese online retailers Shein or Temu.

The fine is the third handed out under the Digital Services Act. The first two were given to Temu and X, and Shein is being investigated under the act.

The act allows for fines of up to 6% of a company's revenue, but as Alibaba had a global turnover of €122bn last year, the penalty is far short of that.

Henna Virkkunen, executive vice president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy at the commission, said that "scale is not an excuse" for selling dangerous or illegal goods.

"The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online - it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations," she said.

AliExpress said: "We disagree with today's decision and the disproportionate fine, which does not adequately reflect our established framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made.

"We are carefully reviewing the decision and considering all available options."

The company now has to pay the fine and present a plan to the EU by 20 October that includes what action it will take to tackle the breaches.

Earlier this year, Temu was given a €200m fine for allowing the sale of illegal products such as dangerous baby toys.
